coredata.dc:title: Synthesis, characterization and bioimaging application of laser-ablated graphene-oxide nanoparticles (nGOs)
coredata.dc:creator: Yogesh, Gaurav Kumar
Shuaib, E.P.
Roopmani, Purandhi
Gumpu, Manju Bhargavi
Krishnan, Uma Maheswari
Sastikumar, D.
coredata.dc:description: Graphene oxide nanoparticles (nGOs) were prepared by nanoscond pulsed laser ablation (ns-PLA) of graphene solution at a wavelength of 1064 nm. The High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) reveals the average particles size of nGOs as 26 nm with an interplanar spacing of 0.21–0.24 nm indicating the crystalline nature of nGOs. X-ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis exhibited peak at 14.4° suggesting the exfoliation of graphene sheet under the PLA of graphene solution. Raman, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopic (XPS) analysis reveals the oxidation of graphene precursor. Photoluminescence (PL) studies of nGOs showed the excitation-dependent PL emission with peak PL emission intensity in the bluish-green region (455 nm). In-vitro studies indicate that synthesized nGOs at a low and high concentration (1, 5 and 10% v/v) showed no direct cytotoxicity on smooth muscle cells at different time points. Moreover, they do not affect the proliferation rate of smooth muscles cells. Herein, nGOs prepared by PLA was found to be photoluminescent and biocompatible, which can be utilized for bioimaging applications.

head.ce:title: Synthesis, characterization and bioimaging application of laser-ablated graphene-oxide nanoparticles (nGOs)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene oxide nanoparticles (nGOs) were prepared by nanoscond pulsed laser ablation (ns-PLA) of graphene solution at a wavelength of 1064 nm. The High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) reveals the average particles size of nGOs as 26 nm with an interplanar spacing of 0.21–0.24 nm indicating the crystalline nature of nGOs. X-ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis exhibited peak at 14.4° suggesting the exfoliation of graphene sheet under the PLA of graphene solution. Raman, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopic (XPS) analysis reveals the oxidation of graphene precursor. Photoluminescence (PL) studies of nGOs showed the excitation-dependent PL emission with peak PL emission intensity in the bluish-green region (455 nm). In-vitro studies indicate that synthesized nGOs at a low and high concentration (1, 5 and 10% v/v) showed no direct cytotoxicity on smooth muscle cells at different time points. Moreover, they do not affect the proliferation rate of smooth muscles cells. Herein, nGOs prepared by PLA was found to be photoluminescent and biocompatible, which can be utilized for bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene-based nanomaterials have been explored for biomedical, bioimaging, drug delivery and cancer therapy applications due to their unique physiochemical properties [1–6]. Single or few-layer graphene nanomaterials, such as graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), and graphene nanosheet (GNs) are major form of graphene with a wide range of applications [2,3,7]. They are superior among various other carbon materials due to their larger surface area and excellent solubility in various solvents [7]. The hydrogen bonding between the surface and polar functional groups of GO gives an excellent dispersibility and hydrophilicity [8]. Moreover, the outstanding surface activity of GO, which is most likely to be caused by functional groups on the surface of GO can exert adsorptive capability of drugs, biomolecules and growth factors leading to in-vitro cell imaging applications [8]. However, several other studies show the potential cytotoxicity of GO. Thus researchers found that toxicity of GO depends on the complex interplay of several physiochemical properties such as shape, size, oxidation methods, dispersion rate, amount of dose administration, exposure time and synthesis route [2,6].

GO was primarily synthesized by various oxidation methods such as Brodie method [9], Staudenmaier method [10], Hofmann method [11,12], and modified Hummer's method [13]. In view of synthesis of GO, the conventional method suffers from various disadvantageous, such as time-consuming process, poor control over size and production of toxic gases (NO2, N2O4 and ClO2) [14,15]. In this regard, nanosecond pulsed laser ablation (ns-PLA) is considered as green, cheap and versatile technique due to its tight liquid confinement for laser ablation, which neither requires an ultra-high vacuum or inert atmosphere nor it generates any toxic by-product in the end [16–18].

In recent years, ns-PLA technique utilized to synthesize different kind of carbon based nanomaterials (CNMs) such as carbon nanodiamond (CNDs) [19,20], carbon dots (CDs), carbon nanoparticles, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and graphene oxide quantum dots [17,21,22] and graphene nanosheet (GNs) [23] from solid and pelletized graphite target [16,24], micron-size graphite and graphene powder [25], glassy and activated carbon powder [18,26]. Recent studies show that the characteristics of fluorescent CNMs are also affected by nature of solvent use in ns-PLA in solution [25,27,28]. Hence, the synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials by ns-PLA in liquid can be categorized into three depending upon the choice of solvent medium. CDs and GQDs synthesized in distilled/deionized water possess very limited fluorescent emission characteristics and further functionalization (attachment of oxygenous functional groups) was performed by using UV irradiation or acid-treatment [27] to enhance the fluorescence characteristics. While CDs, GQDs and GOQDs were synthesized under the organic solvent like ethanol, acetone and isopropyl alcohol possess exceptionally high fluorescent properties [16,25], but in-vitro cytotoxicity analysis or bioimaging applications is limited, due to disinfectant nature of these organic solvents. CDs/GQDs synthesized under PEG solvent exhibits excellent fluorescent characteristics and show a successful demonstration of in-vitro cytotoxicity analysis on the MCF-7 cancer cell and A549 lung cancer cell lines [18,29].

Therefore, it is essential to establish an approach in ns-PLA technique to overcome the challenges involved in synthesizing the non-toxic fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterial by using an organic solvent like ethanol, acetone and isopropyl alcohol. Hence, in the present study, an additional post-ablation process is utilized after the conventional ns-PLA in solution to obtain a non-toxic and fluorescent nGOs for bioimaging application.

Fig. 1 demonstrates the schematic diagram of the pulsed laser ablation of a graphene solution. The detailed setup designed was discussed in our previous studies [30]. In this experiment, the colloidal solution of (1 mg/mL) was prepared by stirring 80 mg of submicron-sized graphene powder in 80 mL of ethanol (Fig. 2a). An unfocused beam (0.95 cm diameter) of Nd:YAG pulsed laser with a wavelength of 1064 nm was used to irradiate graphene suspension under laser fluence 0.92 J-cm− 2 (650 mJ per pulse energy). After 30 min of laser irradiation, a transparent yellow colour colloidal solution was achieved, which confirms the complete ablation of graphene (Fig. 2b). Then the ablated graphene solution sample was dried at room temperature for 48 h to remove the ethanol solvent to recover GO in powder form. Subsequently, powdered GO was dispersed in double distilled water and ultrasonicated for 30 min to achieve water-dispersible GO (Fig. 2c). Fig. 2c shows a snapshot of GO solution obtained after ultrasonication, and the GO slurry sample was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min to separate the bulk GO particles from graphene oxide nanoparticles (nGOs) (Fig. 3d). Finally, the obtained colloidal nGOs was sent for characterization and bio-imaging analysis.

The optical absorption spectra of nGOs were recorded by using a double beam spectrometer (JASCO, Model: V-670) from 200 to 700 nm. The photoluminescent spectra of CNPs were carried out by using the spectrofluorometer (Horiba, Model: Fluoromax-4), and the sample was excited under various wavelengths ranging from 320 to 420 nm. Raman spectroscopy was used for structural analysis of graphene and nGOs with 532 nm continuous laser light as an excitation source (Horiba, Model: LabRAM HR evolution). The spectrum was recorded from 400 to 4000 cm− 1 to confirm the oxidation of graphene during the laser ablation. The presence of various functional groups on the surface of nGOs was characterized by using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer, Model: Spectrum 100) with KBr as mulling agent. The spectrum was recorded from 400 to 4000 cm− 1. A thin sample was prepared on a glass slide using the drop-casting method to investigate the different chemical composition by using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific, Model: ESCLAB xi, MgKα 400 W, 1253.6 eV x-ray source). Wide-angle X-ray diffraction XRD (Brucker, Model: Ts55, Germany) for graphene powder and nGOs were recoded with Cu K
α radiation λ = 1.54056 Å. Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) (JEOL Model: JSM, 6701F) analysis was carried out to understand the morphological features of graphene powder before laser ablation. High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopic (HRTEM) (JEOL 2010, Model: JSM, 200 kV) analysis of nGOs was performed to evaluate the average particles sizes. A few drops of nGOs was placed on the carbon-coated copper grid and allowed to evaporate at room temperature before analysis.

Human vascular smooth muscle cells (hVSMC) were maintained in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) (Gibco, USA) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS; Gibco, USA) and 1% penicillin-streptomycin (P/S, Gibco, USA) at 37 °C and incubated in 5% CO2 (NU-8500, Nuaire, USA). Tissue culture polystyrene (TCPS) without the addition of nGOs was used as a control. Adhered cells were treated with three concentrations (1, 5 and 10% v/v) of nGOs, respectively. The viability of cells was tested after 6 and 24 h of incubation with nGOs. Cell morphology was imaged at each time point using phase-contrast microscope (Zeiss AxioVert A1, USA). MTS (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-5-(3-carboxymethoxyphenyl)-2-(4-sulfophenyl)-2H-tetrazolium) assay was carried out to quantify the viable cells. Briefly, the samples were washed with DPBS (Dulbecco's phosphate-buffered saline) to remove non-adhered cells and then 500 μL of serum-free media (DMEM without 10% FBS) was added. MTS reagent (50 μL) (Promega, USA) was added to each sample well and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. The reaction was stopped by adding 10% sodium dodecyl sulphate (SDS) solution and the absorbance was recorded at 490 nm in a multi-mode reader (Infinite 200 M, Tecan, Austria). To study the cell uptake of nGOs, 50,000 cells per well were seeded. nGOs (1% v/v) were added to the cultured cells. After 6 and 24 h of incubation, cells were fixed using 4% paraformaldehyde and imaged under 60× oil immersion using laser scanning confocal microscope (FV1000, Olympus, Japan).

Fig. 3a-b shows the FE-SEM images of graphene powder before the laser ablation indicating the presence of poly-disperse and crumbled graphene sheets with size ranging from several hundred nanometers to few micrometers. Similarly, HR-TEM images of graphene revealed the crumpled nature (Fig. 3c). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of graphene sheet exhibited the hexagonal symmetry with Bernal (AB) stacking [31–36]. The measured distance (D) between two reflection points in SAED pattern was found to be around 8.747 nm− 1, which is close to lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to (100) plane of graphitic facet (Fig. 3d) [31,33–38]. The SAED pattern also reveal the two set of hexagonal diffraction pattern, indicating a very small relative twist angle (θ ≈ 8) in between the AB stacked graphene layer [39,40]. Thus SAED pattern reveals that the graphene sheet is well oriented with each other [39].

HRTEM images of nGOs showed a wide range of particles size distribution, which ranges from 10 to 60 nm with an average size particle size is found to be around 26 nm (Fig. 4a-b). The average particle size of nGOs is calculated by statistical evaluation of various particles from TEM images by using ImageJ software. The HRTEM image of nGOs showed the lattice spacing of 0.21–0.24 nm, which is in larger consistent with (100) plane for graphitic facet (0.21 nm) (Fig. 4c) [33]. A slight variation in lattice spacing of (100) plane may arise due to intercalation of –OH groups in graphene layers [33,38]. However, several other researchers also observed similar kind of lattice spacing expansion of (100) plane for GQDS and GOQDs [33–36,41–43]. The SAED pattern of nGOs revealed that misorientation of hexagonal structure, which implies that functional groups protruding the GO layer, which decouple the interaction between the backbones of the neighbouring layer (Fig. 4d) [7].

In Fig. 5 . XRD pattern of nGOs (after ablation) was compared with graphene precursor (before ablation). The XRD pattern of graphene exhibited peaks around 26.5°, 42.3°, 44.5°, 54.6° and 77.4° respectively, which corresponds to the (002), (100), (101), (004) and (110) planes of the hexagonal graphitic carbon structure. The inset of Fig. 5 showed (002) basal plane reflection of well order graphene structure centered at 26.5° corresponding to lattice spacing of 0.335 nm, which is comparable with literature data (JCDPS 89-7213, d(002) = 0.335 nm) (Fig. 6a) [44]. Under the chemical oxidation of graphene, diffraction peak at 26.5° disappeared and a new peak was observed at 2θ ≈ 9 − 11°, with an interplanar spacing around ~0.83 − 0.74 nm [1,45,46]. This increase in inter-planar spacing for graphene oxide is due to intercalated water molecules trapped in between the adjacent layer of graphene oxide [14,45,46]. But in the present case, a broad and low-intensity peak was observed at 2θ= 14.4°, which corresponds to the interplanar spacing of 0.61 nm (Fig. 6b). Previously, several other researchers also observed the similar peak shift in XRD experiment of GO at higher value of 2θ ≅12 ° − 17°[46–51], suggesting the interplanar distance of GO get varied with amount of absorbed water molecules, with values from 0.61 nm for “dry” GO (2θ ≅13 ° − 14°) to 1.2 nm for “hydrated” GO (2θ ≅8 ° − 9°) [7,52] signifying the obtained GO by laser ablation of graphene solution is dry-GO.

The appearance of small peaks at 2θ≈ 21.6° and 23.9° corresponds to lattice spacing of 0.41 nm and 0.37 nm, respectively, implying that the nGOs may comprise with randomly oriented graphitic plates, defects, folding, sp., sp2, and sp3 hybridization carbon structures [32,47,53]. Peak at 2θ = 27.4°with minimal intensity corresponds to the contracted lattice spacing of (002) plane of graphitic carbon. This lattice space contraction commonly observed in nanoscale regime of carbon structure [54,55].

XPS survey scan of both graphene powder (precursor) and synthesized nGOs have been investigated. The XPS compositional analysis shows that graphene powder largely contains the graphitic like carbon (sp2 carbon structure) with a little amount of oxygen bonded carbon, whereas nGOs are mainly composed of sp2 carbon structure along with nitrogen and oxygen bonded carbon with minimal traces of sodium as well. XPS analysis of graphene exhibits only two peaks at the binding energy of 284 eV and 530 eV, which corresponds to C 1s of sp2 carbon and O 1s oxygenous functional groups respectively (Fig. 7a) [7,14]. The elemental scan of C 1s is deconvoluted into three segments at the binding energies of 284.4, 285.3 and 288.0 eV corresponding to sp2 hybridized carbon skeleton (C=C), oxygenous functional group (C-OH/C-O-C) and CO respectively [7,45,49] (Fig. 7b). Singlet O 1s peak is also deconvoluted into three peaks at a binding energy of 531.4, 532.9 and 534.9 eV, which correspond to the oxygenous functional groups like CO, CO and O-C=O attached on or within the surface of graphene [56–58] (Fig. 7c). XPS analysis of nGOs showed three low intensities peaks were observed at the binding energies of 200, 399 and 476 eV corresponding to the C 12p, N 1s and Na 1s peaks respectively (Fig. 8a). The major peaks at 284 and 530 eV, corresponding to the C 1s of carbon skeleton and O 1s of oxygenous functionalities (Fig. 8a). The partial deconvolution of C 1s spectrum is fragmented into five peaks at binding energies of 284.3 eV, 285.5, 286.2, 287.5 and 289.4 eV attributed to the non-oxygenous carbon skeleton (C=C) along with CN, CO, CO and HO-C=O functional groups [38,56], respectively. Deconvoluted peaks signified the presence of abundant amount of oxygenous functional groups on both edges as well as basal plane of GO sheet (Fig. 8b). The singlet O 1s spectrum was deconvoluted into four components at binding energies of 530.6, 532, 533.4 and 535.4 eV, respectively, which corresponds to C=O/N=O, CO and O-C=O functional groups respectively on nGOs [14] (Fig. 8c). A shoulder peak extended in O 1s at 535.4 eV arises due to overlapping of auger Na with O 1s peak. Similarly, the peak positioning at binding energies at 497 eV shows the characteristics of Na 1s in nGOs. The N 1s segment of XPS survey scan is deconvoluted into three peaks, the binding energies of 398.0 to 399.3 eV, attributed due to pyridinic nitrogen attached at the edge of graphitic structure or bonded next to two carbon atoms with a vacancy [59,60] (Fig. 8d). While the binding energy at 401.0 eV corresponds to the pyrolic type of N atoms (C=N) has been bonded to nGOs. The relative abundance of functional groups on nGOs is concurrent with previously reported results for the synthesis of GO [14].

The ratio of the intensity of non-oxygenous carbon (C=C) to the sum of oxygenous carbon (C=O/C-O/N-O) for graphene and nGOs was found to be around 3.72 and 1.21, confirming the oxidation of graphene under ns-PLA. The phenolic functional groups in nGOs were deconvoluted into four peaks and the ratio of hydroxyl/epoxy functional groups to carbonyl/carboxylic functional groups was found to be around 1.66, confirming the presence of larger percentage of oxygenous functionalities on a basal plane than on edges of nGOs (Fig. 8c). The ratio of pyrolic-N to pyridinic-N was found to be 0.37, endorsing the association of nitrogen with carbon with single bond than doubly bonded carbon structure network (Table 1 ).

Raman spectroscopy is a non-destructive technique that provides an insight of electron-phonon interaction, which can be imply to study the electronic and crystallographic structure of carbon materials [61]. Fig. 9a shows a typical Raman spectrum of graphene that possesses three major bands in between the 1100 to 3300 cm− 1 [61]. Firstly, D band at ~1335 cm− 1 is arisen due to out of plane A1g breathing mode of sp2 carbon. It was attributed to disorder or defects in graphitic carbon. Secondly, the G band centered at ~1565 cm− 1 was associated with first-order E2g phonon at the center of Brillion zone of graphite and in-plane stretching vibration of CC bond [62]. Finally, the 2D band positioned at ~2700 cm− 1 corresponds to a fingerprint of graphitic structure. The peak position, shape and intensity of 2D relative to G band reveal the number of graphene layers [62,63]. Fig. 9b displays the presence of additional bands: D’, D”, D + D’, D + G and 2D band arising from breathing mode of D band of carbon rings activated by defects via., double resonance process.

Fig. 9c showed the deconvoluted first-order band of graphene, which exhibits the D, G and D’ band centered at 1338, 1565 and 1598 cm− 1. Besides nGOs also exhibited D” band along with D, G and D’ band, positioned at 1212, 1392, 1576 and 1639 cm− 1. In the present study, D band for graphene was appeared at shorter wavenumber 1338 cm− 1 against the usual position 1350 cm− 1. However, several other researchers have also reported a similar peak position for D band at 1335 cm− 1 [20,64–67]. The intensity of D band relative to G band was associated with the number of defects present in the graphitic structure. It was also observed that a small peak appeared at ~1600 cm− 1, overlapped with G-band of both graphene and nGOs and was commonly referred as D’ band (Fig. 9b-c) [7,64]. This D’ band was usually inactive and become active in defects induced carbon material due to phonon confinement caused by the defects [7].

Fig. 6e-f showed the deconvoluted second-order bands of graphene and nGOs in 2400 to 3300 cm− 1 region of Raman spectra. The D + D’ peak (2481 cm− 1) was observed only in nGOs due to the overtone of D and D’ band. The 2D band centered at ~2700 cm− 1 was the overtone of D band activated by double resonance process, which requires two phonon wave vectors to ensure the conservation of momentum conservation [7,61]. In contrary to D band, 2D band does not require defects for momentum conservation of phonon. The 2D band of graphene and nGOs display three deconvoluted peaks, suggesting the multilayer structure of graphene and nGOs [23,63]. This amount of increase in intensity of three disorder peaks of nGOs can be related to disruption of graphitic structure or introduction of defect/disorder due to ns-PLA of graphene in solution: D band which scatter from K to K’ intervalley; D’ band which scatter from K to K intervalley and D + G band, a combination scattering peak [62,68]. The 2D’ peak positioned at 3125 and 3257 cm− 1 was attributed to the overtone of G band and peak centered at 2975 cm− 1 was combined overtone of G and D band and was commonly referred as D + G band, respectively [62].

Some of the useful parameters evaluated from the fitted Raman spectrum are listed in Table 2 . In the current work, D and G band of nGOs was shifted to higher wavenumber in comparison to peak location of graphene. This study is in good agreement with previously reported results of Raman studies by Reyes et al. for laser ablation of graphite in acetone. The FWHM of nGOs was significantly wider than graphene, which was due to the low-crystallinity or introduction of defects in graphene structure. The fall in ID/IG ratio from 0.75 to 0.21 indicated the decrease in crystalline size under the pulsed laser ablation [16,23].

FTIR spectrum showed the characteristic bands arising due to graphene oxide functional groups which were in agreement with previous reports for graphene oxide synthesized by chemical methods (Fig. 10a) [7,45,69]. The absorption band at 3420 and 1380 cm− 1 may be attributed to the stretching and bending vibration of hydroxyl (-OH) substituent, respectively. The absorption band at 1280 and 1085 cm− 1 arise due to the stretching vibration of alkoxy/epoxy (CO and C-O-C) functional groups [45,49,69–72]. The weak band at 2920 cm− 1 arouse due to -C-H stretching vibration [7,71,72]. The presence of carbonyl functionalities in the sample was evidenced from the stretching vibration at 1750 cm− 1, while the characteristic stretching vibration of graphitic carbon skeleton (C=C) appeared as a strong band at 1630 cm− 1, which confirm the formation of GO after the pulsed laser ablation in solution. A similar observation was also recorded for graphene oxide samples synthesized by chemical routes [14,45,46,70–73].

The absorption spectrum of nGOs showed absorption band around 250 nm that arouse due to π − π∗ transitions of CC bonds of graphitic carbon and a shoulder which appeared at 350 nm due to n − π∗ transitions of CO bonds of oxidized carbon (carbonyl groups) (Fig. 10b) [69,73,74].

Excitation dependent PL emission was observed in nGOs, as the excitation wavelength changes from 280 to 440 nm. The PL peak position was shifted monotonically from 430 to 510 nm and its PL emission intensity increased rapidly at initial stages and later it decreased (Fig. 10c). It was observed that the nGOs exhibited a bright bluish-green colour at 450 nm when excited at 360 nm, which was similar to other GOQDs synthesized by other researchers [17]. The PL emission in nGOs originated from the radiative recombination of electron-hole pair, which was localized within the small sp2 hybridized cluster embedded into sp3 matrix [17,75,76]. Thus electron makes the transition from highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) in each isolated sp3 hybridized carbon cluster (Fig. 11 ). The peak intensity of nGOs increased from 280 nm to 360 nm and subsequently decreased from 360 to 460 nm. This trend appeared due to the presence of a broad size distribution of sp2 hybridized nanodomains, where the fraction of larger sized nanodomains leads to red-shift in PL emission [77,78].

However, the quantum confinement of an individual sp2 hybridized carbon nanodomains resulted in band lying in the visible range of the spectrum [77,78]. When such nanodomains were irradiated under specific wavelength, only a certain kind of nanodomains dominate the PL emission. Therefore, due to the broad size distribution of sp2 hybridized nanodomains, the fraction of larger sized nanodomains resulted in multiple emission and redshift of the peak (Fig. 11) [78,79]. Further, the full-width half maxima (FWHM) of each PL band was about 100 nm. The nGOs showed PL emission peak at 440 nm when excited at 340 nm, and the observed results were in good agreement with earlier reports on GOQDs synthesized by chemical route [17,80].

Fig. 14 demonstrated the bioimaging potential for nGOs when tested at two different laser wavelengths (405 nm and 488 nm) of a confocal microscope. After 6 h of incubation, a uniform distribution of bright blue fluorescence was observed in the nucleus and green fluorescence in cytoplasm of the cells. This confirmed the readily penetrating tendency of nGOs into the cells and reached the nucleus within the 6 h of incubation. However, after 24 h of incubation, nGOs were found specifically near the nuclear membrane. The green fluorescence diminishes, due to the diffusion of small portion of graphene oxide through the nuclear pore and was concentrated near the nuclear membrane. Overall results that indicate nGOs were exceptionally non-toxic photoluminescent nanoparticles with good infiltration rate and fast migration in the vascular cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism for laser ablation of graphene
ce:section.ce:para:
Laser-beam interaction of microscopic particles typically depends on the size of the laser pulse. Thus the duration of pulse width determines the possible mechanism for laser ablation in solution. In the case of ns-PLA, the pulse width (τ
P ) is much larger than both electron-phonon relaxation time (τ
e−ph ) and phonon-phonon relaxation time (τ
ph−ph ). The typical interaction time duration for ns-PLA using Nd:YAG laser is as follows: τ
ph−ph ≪τ
e−ph ≪τ
P [84,85]. The principle of ns-PLA in the liquid is based on photothermal fragmentation of bulk materials. The incoming laser pulse energy (photon) absorbed by the electronic system of graphene was easily transmitted to lattice within a few picoseconds due to high thermal conductivity of graphene. In a few picoseconds, a thermal-equilibrium was achieved between the electronic system and lattice system, thus all the energy pile-up at the lattice system. Hence, the temperature of the lattice system attains such an extreme temperature that favoured fragmentation of bulk carbon material into smaller fragments [18,84,86]. The solvents surrounding the surface of carbon precursor explosively transform into vapours (composed of decomposed ethanol and oxygenous moieties) [84]. Subsequently, these oxygenous moieties get trapped in between the graphene layer and attached on the surface (basal-plane) of the graphene layer. Hence, with each incoming laser pulse, the graphene sheet gets further get fragmented and oxidized until the graphene sheet reduced to the nano-scale regime.

The present work demonstrates a successful synthesis of non-toxic fluorescent nGOs using laser ablation of graphene solution. The results of spectroscopic analysis of nGOs showed the characteristics peak of graphene oxide indicating the presence of GO in the synthesized sample. HRTEM and SAED pattern analysis indicated the crystalline nature of the sample. The photoluminescence studies showed nGOs bright blue-green emission in the range of 430–510 nm when excited under UV region of the spectrum. In-vitro studies demonstrated the nGOs has no cytotoxic effect on human vascular smooth muscles cells and there is no significant reduction in cell viability even at high concentration. The nGOs internalized in the cells within 6 h without affecting the PL emission indicating its photostability in the biological milieu. Overall, our results indicate that the laser ablation technique is a simple and reliable method for the preparation of fluorescent non-toxic nGOs that can be explored further for biomedical applications. The cytocompatibility of nGOs can be attributed to the heavy metal ion-free synthesis protocol as well as the non-usage of stabilizing agents during the course of the experiment. It is clear that the method of preparation is a key factor that influences the toxicity of graphene-based nanomaterials.

Gaurav Kumar Yogesh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. E.P. Shuaib: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Purandhi Roopmani: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Manju Bhargavi Gumpu: Formal analysis. Uma Maheswari Krishnan: Investigation, Formal analysis. D. Sastikumar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.
